dek: Aspen is what happens when a place with some of the most private wealth in America actually spends it in public — and it scores 85.5, the highest in the pilot, precisely because that spending is real.

sections.heading: A public realm that punches far above its 17,000 people
body: Pitkin County has fewer than 17,000 residents, and it acts like a place ten times its size. The Public Realm pillar is where that shows: Pitkin County Open Space & Trails conserves roughly 30,000 acres of voter-funded land, the City of Aspen holds about 1,100 acres more, and around 85% of residents live within a ten-minute walk of a park or trailhead. The library circulates roughly 237,000 items a year to a service population of about 13,000 — nearly 18 items per person, a figure most cities never approach.

The Civic Fabric pillar is just as strong. There are 38 National Register listings in the county (about 22 per 10,000 residents) and something like a dozen serious cultural institutions — the Aspen Art Museum, the Wheeler Opera House, the Aspen Music Festival & School, the Aspen Institute — for a resident base that would barely fill a mid-sized high school. The walkable Victorian downtown, which we assessed but did not field-survey, sits near the top of our town-center scale at 95. Free in-town transit, a compact historic grid, and heavy investment in trails round it out. This is a small mountain county carrying a global-tier public life.

sections.heading: Health, schools, and a tax base doing real work
body: The Health & Safety numbers are among the best in the country. Life expectancy runs around 86.8 years — Pitkin consistently ranks near the top of every longevity list — poverty sits near 7%, and violent crime, once you strip out the inflated aggregator rates that a tiny population and a swelling tourist crowd produce, lands around 1.8 per 1,000. Education matches: 62.8% of adults hold a bachelor's degree or higher, well above Colorado's 45.7% and the national 35.7%, feeding a small, high-resource school district that tests well above grade level.

What makes this more than a rich-town story is the Stewardship pillar — the evidence that private money is actually being turned into shared, durable assets. The county's 2024 budget proposed about $40.9 million in capital spending — deed-restricted housing, a clean-energy microgrid, airport and landfill infrastructure — which pencils out to roughly $2,409 per resident in a single year. The all-funds budget climbed from about $216 million in 2024 toward a proposed $311 million for 2026. Drinking water comes largely from designated wilderness on Castle and Maroon creeks and stayed in compliance with health-based standards. This is a place converting concentrated wealth into public wealth on purpose, not by accident.

sections.heading: The honest caveats
body: Three things keep this from being a simple triumph, and they matter. First, the population is tiny, so almost every per-capita figure here is volatile — a handful of bridges, a few crimes, one lumpy capital year can swing a number hard, and several of our metrics are modeled rather than directly measured. Second, the income data understates the truth in the other direction: median household income reads as $102,645, but that ACS wage-and-salary figure misses most of what Aspen actually holds. The real signal is the median home value of about $1.14 million, among the highest in the nation, and the large share of property owned by part-time or non-resident owners who never show up in the resident income line.

Third, and most important: this generous public realm is enabled by that second-home wealth, and to a real degree serves it. Open space, culture, and pristine downtown are amenities that make the place more valuable to the people who can already afford it, even as extreme housing costs strain the working families who keep the resort running — which is exactly why the county is spending on deed-restricted housing. The success is genuine. It is also entangled with the inequality that funds it. We did not field-survey Pitkin County, so read these as a careful outside read, not a walked one.

sections.heading: Why it leads the pilot
body: Most wealthy places show a large gap between what residents own privately and what the public shares. Pitkin County closes that gap — its extreme private wealth is matched, and by our accounting slightly exceeded, by genuine public wealth: the open space, the transit, the institutions, the schools, the capital investment. That is what earns the pilot's top score of 85.5. The honest footnote is that Aspen is a hard model to copy, because the wealth doing the funding is not distributed the way the public goods are. But as proof that a community can spend real money in common rather than only in private, it's the clearest example we have.
